original_hypothesis.title: ASDUSDC.recover() can mint asdUSDC against arbitrary ERC20 balances (non-whitelisted), enabling supply inflation and reserve dilution if owner is compromised or makes an error
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ASDUSDC.recover() mints asdUSDC equal to (token.balanceOf(this) - usdcBalances[token]) scaled by decimals, without requiring the token to be whitelisted or to be an actual USDC variant. This creates an admin mint surface where asdUSDC supply can be inflated against worthless tokens, diluting legitimate depositors and potentially enabling withdrawals of real USDC versions if liquidity exists.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is an explicit invariant breach (Phase A) and a realistic failure mode under owner compromise or operational mistake. Even if 'recover' is intended for only genuine USDC, code does not enforce that intent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: recover lacks require(whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion]); mints based on arbitrary token.balanceOf

preconditions: Owner calls recover() on a non-USDC or malicious token address
Contract holds some balance of that token (airdropped or transferred in)
There exists withdrawable liquidity in a real whitelisted USDC version for the owner to extract value

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No whitelist gating; no token registry; no cap

analysis_questions: Is recover intended to work only for whitelisted versions? (docs imply yes)
Could third parties grief by sending dust tokens to enable accidental over-mint via recover?
Does protocol rely on asdUSDC being fully backed for DEX swaps/liquidity?
validation_requirements: Simulate calling recover() on an arbitrary ERC20 and then withdrawing real USDC to see dilution path
Review operational controls around owner key management and recover usage
Consider restricting recover to whitelisted tokens and/or adding explicit version registry

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If owner is compromised, attacker sends a worthless ERC20 to ASDUSDC, calls recover(worthless), receives newly minted asdUSDC, then withdraws a real whitelisted USDC version until reserves are depleted, leaving legitimate users with unredeemable asdUSDC.
prerequisites: Owner key compromise or malicious owner behavior

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Material loss of real USDC reserves held by ASDUSDC

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests ensuring recover is whitelist-restricted

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain mint events to owner without corresponding USDC deposits

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
